mission.title: Install and Configure Kitops on Kubernetes
mission.description: KitOps is an open source DevOps tool for packaging and versioning AI/ML models, datasets, code, and configuration into an OCI Artifact. Installing KitOps allows you to manage your AI/ML projects efficiently and securely, leveraging the power of the CNCF ecosystem.

steps.title: Add the KitOps Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official KitOps Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add kitops https://charts.kitops.org
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install KitOps using Helm
steps.description:
Install KitOps in a new namespace called 'kitops' with the following command:
```bash
helm install kitops kitops/kitops --namespace kitops --create-namespace --version v1.11.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the KitOps pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kitops
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Update the KitOps deployment to set resource limits. Create a YAML file named `kitops-resources.yaml`:
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: kitops
  namespace: kitops
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: kitops
          resources:
            limits:
              cpu: "500m"
              memory: "512Mi"
``` 
Then apply the configuration:
```bash
kubectl apply -f kitops-resources.yaml
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
Forward the KitOps service to your local machine to access the UI:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/kitops --namespace kitops 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of KitOps will have the KitOps pods running in the 'kitops' namespace, and you will be able to access the KitOps UI at http://localhost:8080. Resource limits will be configured as specified.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kitops https://charts.kitops.org
helm repo update
helm install kitops kitops/kitops --namespace kitops --create-namespace --version v1.11.0

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: kitops
  namespace: kitops
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: kitops
          resources:
            limits:
              cpu: "500m"
              memory: "512Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the KitOps release from the Kubernetes cluster using Helm. This will remove the deployment and associated resources but not the CRDs or persistent volumes.
```bash
helm uninstall kitops --namespace kitops
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with KitOps to ensure a clean removal.
```bash
kubectl delete crd kitops.kitops.org
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace kitops
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the KitOps namespace and verify that it has been removed successfully. This will clean up any remaining resources in the namespace.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kitops
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current state of the KitOps release. You can use the following command to export the current configuration.
```bash
helm get values kitops --namespace kitops > kitops-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts and then upgrade KitOps to the desired version. Replace `v1.12.0` with the version you want to upgrade to.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kitops kitops/kitops --namespace kitops --version v1.12.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check the status of the KitOps pods to ensure they are running the new version successfully after the upgrade.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kitops
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the KitOps pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for the specific pod to diagnose the issue.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kitops
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the KitOps service, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly and the service is running.
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace kitops
kubectl port-forward svc/kitops --namespace kitops 8080:80
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If the KitOps deployment fails due to insufficient resources, check the resource requests and limits set in the deployment configuration. Adjust them as necessary.
```bash
kubectl describe deployment kitops --namespace kitops
```

troubleshooting.title: Failed to apply resource limits
troubleshooting.description:
If the resource limits configuration fails to apply, check the YAML syntax and ensure the deployment is correctly defined. Validate the YAML file with:
```bash
kubectl apply -f kitops-resources.yaml --dry-run=client
```

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
